name: emotional scaffolding
top_issues: Opening buries the value: the article opens with a Key Takeaways block and then two scene-setting paragraphs before anything actionable appears. The line 'When a seventh grader freezes during a lab experiment, it's rarely about the science' is vivid, but it's paragraph 3 — not sentence 1. Cut the takeaways box from the top (move to end or sidebar) and lead with that 7th-grade moment or a direct definition.

Wayground value proposition is generic and repeated verbatim twice: 'Wayground offers standards-aligned resources with built-in accommodations that make differentiation effortless' appears word-for-word in both the intro and the closing section. Neither instance names a specific feature (e.g., Create with AI, tiered question sets, accommodation toggles). Teachers reading this cannot picture what Wayground actually does differently from any other edtech tool.

AI-ish phrasing surfaces in several spots: 'makes differentiation effortless,' 'saving teachers time while supporting every student,' and 'focus on what matters most' are the exact phrases teachers associate with marketing copy, not practitioner writing. These appear three times combined and undercut the otherwise solid teacher voice.
positive_signals: The affective readiness table (Observable Sign / What It May Signal / Quick Teacher Move / Differentiation Option) is genuinely excellent — it's the kind of one-page reference a teacher would laminate and keep on a clipboard during labs.

The FAQ section is practical and well-structured: answers like 'Confusion shows up as specific questions… Overload looks like shutdown, silence, or task avoidance' give teachers precise, usable language rather than vague reassurances.
narrative: This article has real bones — the affective readiness table and the FAQ section are legitimately classroom-ready and reflect the kind of nuanced, observable-behavior thinking that experienced teachers recognize as true. However, two issues require revision before publish: the Wayground mentions are copy-pasted marketing language ("makes differentiation effortless" appears twice, with zero feature specificity), which will read as hollow to any skeptical teacher and scores a 2 on C10, triggering an automatic REVISE. The opening also needs restructuring — the strong 7th-grade science scenario is buried in paragraph three behind a bullet block and a definition paragraph, and the intro closer ("Ready to build confidence and curiosity while saving hours each week?") reads like a CTA inserted mid-article rather than earned editorial flow. Fix the opening to lead with the 7th-grade moment, replace both Wayground boilerplate lines with one specific feature call-out (e.g., how Wayground's tiered question builder or accommodation settings connect directly to the affective readiness strategies described), and scrub the three instances of "effortless/saves time/focus on what matters most" phrasing — then this article is close to a clean PASS.
